B. Tech. Courses offered by Physics Department
Course 1: Quantum Mechanics & Waves and Oscillations
Blackbody radiation and the concept of photons, Quantum mechanical operators, Schroedinger
equation, Wave function, Statistical interpretation, Superposition Principle, Continuity equation
for probability density, Stationary states, Bound states, Free-particle solution, 1-D infinite
potential well, Expectation values and uncertainty relations, 1-D finite potential well, Quantum
mechanical tunneling, Kronig-Penny model and emergence of bands.
General introduction to periodic motion, Simple harmonic motion (SHM), Equation of motion for
SHM, Superposition of 2-vibrations, beats, Lissajous figures, Superposition of N-vibrations,
Undamped and damped free vibrations, Forced vibrations, resonance, Practical examples of
resonance, Coupled oscillations, Normal modes for 2-coupled oscillators, Normal modes for
N-coupled oscillators, Fourier transforms and normal mode analysis, Wave equation,
Superposition of waves, Phase and group velocity, Dispersion
Course 2: Quantum Mechanics & Electromagnetic Theory
Poisson’s and Laplace’s equations, Multipole expansion and dipole, Electric and magnetic fields
in a medium, Concept of the vector potential and magnetic moment, Maxwell’s equations,
Boundary conditions, EM wave equation, Plane wave solutions, Poynting vector and intensity of
the EM wave, Fields of moving charges, Lorentz oscillator model, Complex refractive index,
Dispersion, Wave packet, Phase and Group velocities, Fresnel equation: Reflection and
refraction of EM waves at a dielectric interface, EM waves in a conducting medium and plasma,
Concept of Fourier transformation and its application
Blackbody radiation and the concept of the photon, Quantum mechanical operators,
Schrodinger equation, Wave function, Statistical interpretation, Superposition Principle, the
Continuity equation for probability density, Stationary states, Bound states, Free-particle
solution, 1-D infinite potential well, Expectation values and uncertainty relations, 1-D finite
potential well, Quantum mechanical tunneling, Kronig-Penny model and emergence of bands.

Scattering states: n-p and p-p scattering at low energies; effective range and scattering length; singlet and triplet states; ortho- and para-hydrogen, charge independence of nuclear forces. c) Nucleon-nucleon scattering at higher energies d) Polarization in nucleon-nucleon scattering – l.s forces e) Exchange forces and saturation f) General properties of nucleon-nucleon forces Yukawa potential. - Envisioning a research based-teaching environment
- Research for impact: Becoming the trendsetter
- Popularizing Physics - outreach, local challenges

# Page Title: Physics

- Abhishek Kumar
- Assistant Professor
- abhishek.kumar@iitjammu.ac.in

#### Abhishek Kumar

I am a condensed matter theorist interested in exploring dynamical and transport properties of systems away or close to quantum critical point of certain types. I joined IIT Jammu as an Assistant Professor in March, 2025. I look forward to doing exciting science in collaboration with enthusiastic students and postdocs.

**Recent news from the group:**

* Our new work on resonant Edelstein and inverse Edelstein effects, charge-to-spin conversion, and spin pumping from chiral spin modes has been published in **Physical Review B (Perspective)** [DOI: https://doi.org/10.1103/5p54-96d7] as a contribution to a special issue dedicated to the memory of late Emmanuel Rashba.

* Our recent work on a tight-binding and density-functional investigation of the Raman tensor in 2D massive Dirac fermion systems has been published in **Physical Review B** [https://link.aps.org/doi/10.1103/m1p1-w3w4].

##### Research Interest

Quantum polar phase transition

Transport in correlated electron systems

Fermi-liquid with spin-orbit coupling

Theory of Raman, electron spin resonance (ESR) and electric-dipole spin resonance (EDSR) spectroscopies

Strongly correlated systems: cuprates high temperature superconductors, flat-band systems

##### Education Qualification

PhD (Aug 2013 - Aug 2020): University of Florida, Gainesville, USA

Integrated BS-MS (Aug 2008 - Jun 2013): IISER Kolkata, India

##### Professional Background

Assistant Professor, IIT Jammu (Mar 2025 - present)

##### Academic Interests

**Research interests** - Theoretical condensed matter physics

**Teaching interests** - Solid State Physics, Statistical Mechanics, Electromagnetic theory and Quantum Mechanics

##### Teaching Engagements

**Autumn (2025-26) - Semester 25-01:** PH104 (TO) Waves and Oscillations

PH101 (PO) UG Physics laboratory

**Spring (2025-26) - Semester 25-02:** PH103 (TO) Electrodynamics

PH621 (TO) Advanced Mathematical Physics (Multi-faculty course)

##### Research Experience

**Postdoctoral experiences:**

**Florida State University and National High Magnetic Field Lab (Quantum Initiative Postdoctoral Fellow) (Sept 2024 - Mar 2025)**, *Area of work - Dynamical and Transport properties of electronic systems*

**Université de Sherbrooke (Aug 2022 - Sept 2024)**, *Area of work - charge nematicity in cuprates*

**Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey, USA (Sept 2020 - July 2022)**, *Area of work - Polar metals*

##### Publications

**Selected publications:**

1) **Abhishek Kumar**, D. Sénéchal, and A.-M. S. Tremblay, “Cluster dynamical mean-field study of intra-unit-cell charge nematicity in hole-doped cuprates,” Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, vol. 122, no. 10, e2419534122 (2025).

2) **Abhishek Kumar**, V. I. Yudson, and D. L. Maslov, “Quasiparticle and nonquasiparticle transport in doped quantum paraelectrics,” Phys. Rev. Lett., vol. 126, p. 076601 (2021).

3) A. S. Mishchenko, L. Pollet, N. V. Prokof’ev, **Abhishek Kumar**, D. L. Maslov, and N. Nagaosa, “Polaron mobility in the “beyond quasiparticles” regime,” Phys. Rev. Lett., vol. 123, p. 076601 (2019).

4) **Abhishek Kumar**, P. Chandra, and P. A. Volkov, “Phonon-induced collective modes in spin-orbit coupled polar metals,” (Editor’s suggestion) Phys. Rev. B, vol. 108, p. 075162 (2023).

5) **Abhishek Kumar**, P. Chandra, and P. A. Volkov, “Spin-phonon resonances in nearly polar metals with spin-orbit coupling,” Phys. Rev. B, vol. 105, p. 125142 (2022).

6) **Abhishek Kumar** and D. L. Maslov, “Effective lattice model for the collective modes in a fermi liquid with spin-orbit coupling,” (Editor’s suggestion) Phys. Rev. B, vol. 95, p. 165140 (2017).

research institution where teaching and research are integral components of our mission.

courses, as well as pursue Ph.D. studies. Our students are the cornerstone of our
strength.

Within our department, we house a team of highly experienced
and dedicated faculty members who excel both as educators and researchers in their
respective domains. Supported by a proficient group of staff members, we ensure
efficient operations on both technical and administrative fronts. Our driving force lies
in fostering contemporary and fundamental growth across all aspects of our endeavors.

## Master in Science

The main objective of the M.Sc. (Physics) program is to prepare a talent pool of manpower by inducting them right after their bachelor degree and provide them quality education with sound fundamentals to contribute effectively in these rapidly changing times, and in addition train the students to conduct research in a specific stream of interdisciplinary physics. At the end of the second semester, a student will opt for a stream and allocation will be made as per the availability of the seats and the Department policy. All the admitted candidates irrespective of their streams will undergo a common curriculum for the first two semesters that will cover the core physics courses which form the basis for the courses in the interdisciplinary areas.
